danger_signals.visual_form: multiple sprite characters moving toward player from gates/fortresses
danger_signals.meaning: active combat zone; waves of incoming threats

danger_signals.visual_form: distinct enemy sprite that appears at level gates, runs away after ordering troops
danger_signals.meaning: command authority present; tactical threat organizing the assault

danger_signals.visual_form: static environmental structures on screen that spawn enemy units
danger_signals.meaning: enemy stronghold; source of continuous threat generation

danger_signals.visual_form: pits, water, gas rooms visible in level layout
danger_signals.meaning: instant-death environmental dangers; map-based threats independent of enemy action

danger_signals.visual_form: bunker entrances and barriers that visually block progression
danger_signals.meaning: hidden threats contained; resource expenditure required to progress safely

safety_signals.visual_form: POW sprites transported across screen by enemy convoy; can be freed by player
safety_signals.meaning: objective completion markers; non-hostile entities indicating mission success

safety_signals.visual_form: officer character running away after troop dispatch
safety_signals.meaning: optional high-value target; reward for aggressive pursuit rather than danger

safety_signals.visual_form: machine gun upgrade, unlimited grenade power-up, glasses items in underground shelters
safety_signals.meaning: temporary power progression; enhanced capability zones
resource security; extra lives awarded at 10,000 points and per 50,000 thereafter

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: dropped by helicopter at level start; represents lone commando infiltration

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: generic enemy soldier sprites; distinct officer character with command authority
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: cowardly officer who flees after dispatching troops; distinguishes leadership from rank-and-file
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: fortresses, gates, underground bunkers; organized military infrastructure
hidden in underground shelters accessible only via grenade destruction

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: helicopter drop-off at jungle level start; player character Super Joe begins surrounded
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player spawns with unlimited assault rifle and limited grenades; must navigate toward extraction

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: screen transition between four unique level designs that repeat with increasing difficulty across 16 total levels
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: minor visual/layout changes signal escalating threat density and enemy complexity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: screen locks; gate/fortress visual dominates; officer character visible dispatching soldiers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: soldiers stream from gate in waves; officer runs backward; bonus points awarded for shooting officer

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: pits, water, gas rooms rendered in level terrain; some blocked by destructible bunker covers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player cannot survive entering these zones without grenades (for bunkers) or alternate traversal; instant failure state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: machine gun upgrade sprite, unlimited grenade item, special glasses item appear in hidden underground shelters
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player capability increases; upgrades lost upon life loss; glasses item reveals all hidden bunker locations visually

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: secret ending message displays if game cleared with 3 lives and 24 grenades remaining
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: specific resource management requirement signals developer-intended optimal play path

environmental_storytelling.example: four unique areas in NES version that repeat with difficulty scaling; regional text 'BROKE THE ___ AREA NOW RUSH/RASH TO THE ___ AREA' signals continuous territory conquest narrative
fortresses and gates serve dual purpose: visually reinforce enemy military control of territory while functionally acting as spawn points for threat waves
officer character runs away after dispatching troops, visually communicating cowardice and establishing moral hierarchy—player incentivized to pursue and shoot officer for bonus points
underground shelters containing prisoners and power-ups require grenade destruction to access, suggesting enemy fortification and hidden strategic assets
level start visual of player dropped by helicopter establishes commando infiltration scenario without explicit narrative text

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: can fire assault rifle in eight directions with unlimited ammo; grenades throw only vertically regardless of facing direction; grenades can destroy obstacles and clear multiple enemies; movement in all eight directions
machine gun upgrade increases firepower; unlimited grenade upgrade removes resource constraint; glasses item reveals hidden bunker locations; upgrades permanently lost on life loss
instantly dies from pits, water, gas rooms regardless of invulnerability cheat status; must use grenades or alternate routes to survive hazard zones
streams toward player in waves from gates and fortresses; generates continuously during level play; demonstrates organized military assault pattern
appears at level gates; orders soldiers to attack; immediately runs away in retreat; high bonus point target; establishes hierarchy and moral dimension
moves across screen under enemy guard; can be freed by player combat; visual markers of objective completion and moral victory
screen locks in place; no further terrain scrolling; soldier waves emerge continuously from static gate; creates contained arena for final combat test before area transition

notes: Commando (NES 1986) uses overhead perspective to communicate tactical infiltration through environmental layout and enemy spawning patterns. Visual communication is primarily through sprite behavior and environmental fixtures rather than color or detailed faction markings. The game lacks explicit political narrative but implies military conflict through hierarchy (officer vs. soldiers), territory control (areas/gates), and rescue objectives (POW liberation). Japanese title 'Senjou no Ookami' (Wolf of the Battlefield) suggests individual soldier heroism against organized force. Hidden developer messages indicate personal touches (fan messages, birthday codes) that humanize the design team but don't directly impact player-facing manifestation systems. The game's danger/safety dichotomy is primarily mechanical (hazard zones) rather than visual-narrative.
